speaker: interviewer
text: 1995 and we are in Sydney, Australia. OK. Camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it that you finally made your decision to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made my decision in-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] God-- '49 when I was absolutely at loose ends. Newly divorced, having to do something, and enrolling at-- at that stage, there was a possibility to enter university, even as a mature age student, like here. And I realized that I couldn't get in again because my father was a-- an academic. My father was also a lawyer, which was certainly not for first intake for students.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for once in my life, I was determined to make a decision which was contrary to my previous ones. Possibly, it was helped by the fact that my uncle, who was here and who sent me a landing permit again as a single person, made it quite clear that if I wanted to bring my father to Australia-- and by then my stepmother, who is very dear to me and was a very good friend-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOB] I knew there would be a catalyst. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to come first. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was hard because I had to say goodbye. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was very sorry for my father. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] We said goodbye in the hope of me getting him out as soon as I could. And indeed, it was unbelievable because after probably a very happy time en route to Australia, I arrived in Sydney on Australia Day and I was told that offices are closed. But I would have had no trouble getting my father's landing permit immediately. The offices opened after the holiday. My father had been over 40, and that was the cut off age. And I knew that he wanted to come. He was quite prepared to come and join me. And I remember not even taking in anything about Sydney, having the total one track mind of getting the landing permit, which I did in one day. And I think my love affair of Australia started then, because it was such an anti-climactic, efficient, and keeping your promises type of beginning in my life. And we sent the landing permit to my father, and by the time it got there, the Iron Curtain fell down on Czechoslovakia, which was a real shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was probably strengthened by the friendship I had req-- acquired en route to Australia, by the people I had met, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and by the fact that I was absolutely charmed by everything and everybody that I met on first sight. It was another world. I really thought that magic world that I had lived in had come true.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't quite as easy in the beginning as I had thought when I first arrived, but it was be-- but it was certainly a constructive beginning. Did you want to ask me something or do you want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it that your father finally managed to leave Czechoslovakia [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: We were separated again for 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a long wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very long wait, and my father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came out on a visit when I was happily married with two children and one on the way. And he came here for a visit, fell in love with Syndey like I did and have been, and increasingly showed enormous difficulty in facing his return when the time was up. And it was the efforts and the efficiency and the love of my husband who made it possible for my father to get the promise of permanent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stay in Australia, and they promised that his wife will be able to join him. We only had the communist authorities to-- to beat down, and it just worked. I don't know. I'm sure there was some magic, the way my husband handled it. It-- it just happened. My father never went back, my stepmother came out, and if I've ever seen anyone adore life and perhaps be an embodiment of life begins at 50 plus, it was my father. And it made a huge difference to my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you-- going back a little, then, when did you meet and marry your second husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Sydney in 1950. I had a choice of many, many jobs because it was a migrant's paradise then. And I found an office job after a few abortive, dreadful ones to start with. And the people I worked for were very, very kind to me. They were an Austrian couple-- Jewish couple. And they somehow or other were always concerned at my being alone and a loner, very much a loner. And it became pretty obvious that I was hiding in that facade of being happiest on my own. And they arranged for me to come to a party over the Easter-- Jewish Easter weekend, which was to be a no excuse party of many people that wanted to just get together where there was a big house available. And I was at that party that I really had to drag myself to, because I was terribly, terribly difficult mixing with people at that stage. And my husband was one of the guests, and it was love at first sight. And my life just took such a happy turn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married on December 4, 1950. And it was the beginning of a restorative period [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in many ways. Number one, I found my way back to my type of religion, which was observance of the tradition that I saw at home. Until then, I was totally unable to function in that corner, as was Michael, my husband, who had been here during the war and was a soldier and joined the AIF, and certainly didn't have the same experiences that I had. But he had lost a great many relatives and he was not able to live a Jewish life. We very much wanted a family, and we were very, very happy to have three lovely children. And did begin a new life, which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] following on such memories as I had from observance at home. And very much instinctively following in the footsteps of my parents-- my mother, mostly, because my father was less in my childhood, unfortunately, through our separation, than I would have wished. And when he came back from Palestine, he was so shattered that his religion just went by the way-- by the wayside. He could no longer follow religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And apart from the home and the big job of bringing up three children, how other-- in other ways, how did you occupy yourself in these years in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have always been very interested in literature, art, and also in people involvement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was possible for me not to work, and I did a great many voluntary work jobs, which gave me a lot of satisfaction in the Jewish community. Michael and I joined the [INAUDIBLE] brith, and in those days, there was a very strong need for welfare activity, which I always carried out. I had, at long last, the opportunities to catch up with various areas of study. But I retained a fear of anything that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a proper framework. Whenever I had to do a-- an organized activity course, I did it with flying colors, but I was mortified. I sat for entry at university, passed it with flying colors. They waived proper entry subjects on my essay. It was perfectly good enough for me to enter. I was mortified. I retained that, I think, unfortunately, insecurity that I could never overcome. I involved myself a great deal in working with my children and I, in the later years of my husband's life, req-- acquired some office skills and could help him in his work. And that was more or less easy. And of course, I had such a hunger to-- to have family that all these incredible voids I had to fill with being with such family as I had, and wider ones that I acquired through very close friendships. And I do, I really do feel, I was very fortunate to be able to have so many good friends in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I believe now your time is very occupied by your beautiful grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is something that I never dreamed I would be fortunate enough to live to. And I-- I just cannot tell you the marvel that this child presents to me. This incredible challenge and joy. And I feel totally optimistic when I'm seeing him or thinking of him. And it's sort of a circle having come to a-- a round. A full round. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether you want me to say something at this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Certainly. Why don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have lived with tremendous guilt for years. I could never explain why, and I could somehow never get past my being here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think the older I-- I am, the more, at the moment, I can say that I have managed to get past that point. I cannot still say that I feel there was a justification for me being alive rather than those that I have lost and those that perished who were not part of my life, but what I can do now is to face the fact that I am. And within that I am, I have been doing a great deal to do that which I have always wanted to, is to work with children. I have built up something as an SRE teacher that I never dreamed I could manage with my rather shy, sort of basically shy nature. I love children. I really can communicate with them. And I have made it a point, and will be for as long as I can, to try and tell children such as I meet that, if I can overcome what I have overcome, they must not let any obstacle in their way be an obstacle. I also would like at this point to say that I have managed to not live with hatred. And I hope I can leave behind a message for those that will perhaps look or hear or listen to my tape. And that is that we must not punish generations that had nothing to do with their parents' sin forever. There's got to be a point at which young people have to be given a chance to develop their potential. And if generations are going to live with a feeling of guilt, it can only create more anger and more anger, and possibly will backfire. And as Jews today who have got the incredible insurance of having Israel, we, too, have to change our outlook towards those who are now grandchildren of those who tormented us, and let history live on. Let those who come after us learn about history, but learn in the proper sense, and learn from it, as well. And I feel, in my capacity, for as long as I can, I want to work and challenge prejudice of any kind, as much as I can. Because I feel this is a totally destructive road. But there is one great danger in our community, which I would like to ask everybody to face, and that is not to, in our freedom in Australia-- in our Jewish freedom-- become complacent and be totally inward looking. It's a huge mistake. I think our suffering was, to a great extent, due to the fact that generations before us, in a chicken and egg situation, had to live an isolated life, had to live an inward live, had no outlet for any other of their pursuits but to be introspective. That no longer is necessary in the free world of today, particularly in the diaspora in Australia. It is essential that we don't lose sight of the fact that we lived here in a country that welcomed us, that gave us the ability to straighten our backs, to have the ability to look forward and back and see the clarity and the balance. And within that balance, it is my fervent hope that we never lose sight of the debt that we owe the community at large-- the human community-- and compart-- compartmentalize as little as we possibly can. Yes, build Israel, strengthen it. To the last drop of my life, I will, too. But don't lose sight of the fact that we are humans first and Jews second. It is impossible to survive otherwise. That is something that I very strongly want to really plead with. And I am very proud of my own children. I think they have grown up to be that which I hope they should be. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for doing this interview, Mrs. Grinston.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little bit about this photograph, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. It's my parent's wedding photograph and I believe they were one of the most wonderful looking couples. And they were. They were both tall and very handsome. And it was taken in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they married in Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the Jewish [INAUDIBLE]-- sorry, I nearly said church-- Synagogue, of which my mother's father was the rosh [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Oh, sorry, their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Arthur and my mother Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lichtner, L-I-C-H-T-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is in this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows the house I was born in. The house that I could have grown up in and lived in happily ever after. It is a post-war photograph of a very denuded, very dilapidated house that I remember totally differently. There is some-- something I can tell you with honesty-- that I have lived in every one of these apartments, and there were six of them. As you walk up a long driveway, you can see the remnants of a small wall. That's exactly the point, which is the-- more or less the entry to the janitor's cottage where Vera and I were hidden. And that cottage no longer stands. That's the only remnant of it. And in its place, somebody else put a very elegant house now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this photo was actually taken later.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a post-war photograph. The house is untouched. That little round garden in front of it is the same as I remembered it, except for no garden in any way being sort of cultivated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the children shown in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little one you can see is my sister, Vera. We called her Verushka. And the other one is myself. This was the garden we so loved, a magical playground that we were fortunate to have. And to my joy, in this photo we're holding hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how old you two might have been at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think my sister was three, and I was barely five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is shown in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Vera, whom I last saw in Auschwitz when we arrived in 1944. This was the way I remember her. And she looks very serious because she didn't like to be photographed. At that stage of her life, she was about 11 and 1/2, nearing 12 years of age, and full of idealism and full of a dream to make it to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the children in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little one is Vera. The bigger one is myself. I chose this photograph for two reasons. A, it was photographed at my grandparents' home in Sered, which is the very place where we started our journey into hell. And it was a happy memory because it was around Pesach. I don't remember the year exactly. But it also signifies to what an extent we were totally unaware of being Jewish. We wore Austrian dirndls. I did, anyway, and my little sister, an embroidered Czech dress. We felt very much of the community at large, and the irony of the situation that I should be in a dirndl in Sered, where I was to suffer so much for being Jewish later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: There you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the significance of this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is very significant because it shows the grave of my grandmother and grandfather, mother's side. My grandfather was a leading light in law and Judaism in Slovakia. And I have that grave still there, intact, barely touched by time. And I feel always so very, very sad that I have no grave, nowhere to actually sort of visit and associate my memories other than in my own head. In my own subconscious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were shown in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my husband Michael and I. It was taken when we, I suppose, got engaged, although we had no formal engagement, to send home to my father and my stepmother of the man I had met, fallen in love with, and was about to marry. And I-- I think it reflects my feelings for him. We were very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1950, towards the end of 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what is the significance of this drawing?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very great significance. It is-- depicts a meeting of an imagined Jewish group of pioneers, I suppose-- a [NON-ENGLISH] meeting-- which was totally imagined. My little sister imagined herself as being one of these girls in Palestine, where she was going to wear a uniform. She knew very little-- none of us did-- how Zionism works. But this was as much as we knew, that there were youth groups, et cetera. This drawing was saved for me somehow by a lucky quirk of fate. My father preserved it, and here I have to say that my stepmother, who is a wonderful embroider, embroidered all these pictures that my little sister made into a lampshade for me. And they are identical with these sketches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you estimate how old Vera might have been when she drew these?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does this official document refer to?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the very eagerly awaited response to my entry exam into a German-- sorry, into the girl's high school in Bratislava, a very good school. And although I was always poor in maths and had a struggle, I passed the test with flying colors and I walked on cloud nine for one week. Because although this document says I was accepted into the high school, a week later I got a very similar document telling me I was not eligible on grounds of my religion and race.

speaker: interviewer
text: This document is dated the 27th of June, 1940. Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This card is-- has an interesting story attached to it, doesn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It does. It is the ID card that my mother bought for me at tremendous expe-- expense and risk, which was supposed to be, one, for a-- a-- an alias in case I had to leave on false papers. It calls me Eva Sykorova, which was a fabricated name. And it depicts my domicile as Nitre, as a student. And not one of the other bits of official language on it is real. In fact, I remember the man creating the rubber stamp in front of our eyes. It was just unreal. It was a Jewish group of forgers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have the opportunity or the need to use this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I carried it with me against my mother's advice. Once, I-- I entered a movie theater. This was prohibited for Jewish children. I couldn't bear the temptation. It was a Shirley Temple movie. I didn't need to use it, but I ripped off my star, and I guess the guilt burnt in my subconscious for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I believe, is the first page of your diary. It is. It's a diary that is my first sign back to, I guess, a kind of normalcy after I was liberated by the Russian army in Ravensbruck. And if you understood Slovak, you could analyze my lifelong love for food because I'm going into great detail about the three daily menus. It was just so incredible that I could actually get three meals a day again. If I look hard, I see a great deal of cabbage mentioned and rolled oats. But significant is the last line, if you could please concentrate on that. It is phonetic spelling of Russian and says [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], which means, I want to go home. I didn't realize I had none.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a bit obscured, but I believe the fir-- date of the first entry would be the 17th of June, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be right. It was actually written when I started to recover from typhoid, still in Ravensbruck, waiting to be taken back to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the significance of this card, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The significance is that there were, again, ID cards. Somehow we never got by without any. But this one, I think, that you showed first is a Czechoslovakian one already in Russian language, as well. And then there is another one, which is an ID card that I got when I was, I think, leaving for Bratislava from the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from what I can see, a date here, 18th of July, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already after I had been in the TB hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't see the date. It's useless.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it's the 14th of the sixth, 14th of July.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's probably right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this telegram?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a telegram that my father sent from Palestine to my uncle, who was a very important part of my life because he was the man to whom I went when I actually went back to Bratislava. And he was the recipient of the telegram which says, Eva is well. She is in Prague in the Masarykove house, which was the TB hospital where I was staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from what we can make out, the date could be the 14th of July, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be right. My father, through the Red Cross, found out of my having come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older one is my stepmother, who-- whose name is Helen Sylvan, who was a very, very wonderful friend and stand-in mother to me. The younger one is my daughter, Elizabeth, on her wedding day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she get married, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] That was the one question I haven't been prepared for. She's been married for about four years, I think. Four or five-- five years. Five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1990.

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- Around about 1990. I'm really embarrassed. I totally blanked out now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the two people in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, David. My first born, my miracle child, that sort of gave me back the belief that even in the most wrecked body, new life is possible. And I am very, very, very proud of him. The blushing bride is his sister, and I think I can say that that reflection on both their faces is a very, very permanent one. They are very supportive and wonderful friends to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who does this picture show?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my second son, John, who I know doesn't particularly like this photograph. I love it because he's got a huge grin on it. And although I love his new look, and I can't show the long hair because it's not there in this photograph, and I love everything about him in the new look as well, it's this great big grin and his twinkling, lovely eyes which are so special to me. And that's why I wanted to have it on this video.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is my daughter, Elizabeth, and her husband, Simon, the man whom she chose, and chose with such wisdom because I have grown to love and honor him. And I think he is the most wonderful addition to our family and I'm just so thrilled that I could meet him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this charming young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the latest man in my life. The love of my life. Apart from all my other children, he is just something that has given me back such a great deal of joy. And is carrying the name of my late husband, which makes it even more special. And I'm quite sure he will grow up a fantastic human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name is Michael?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was born in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born 18 months ago, and, God willing, won't be an only child for long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape four of the interview with Eva Grinston. It's the 25th of May, 1995. We are in Sydney, Australia, and that's also the end of the whole interview.
